result:
The first thing that strikes me is the hum—low, almost sub-audible—of the refrigerator in the next room. It is the 21st-century hearth, the keeper of leftovers and half-remembered intentions. I think of all the jars I’ve opened, tasted once, and pushed to the back, where they become tiny time capsules: a dab of ghost-pepper jam from a farmers’ market I swore I’d start visiting every Saturday, pesto the color of a summer I didn’t fully live, a plastic ramekin of duck sauce from a Chinese take-out place that closed during the first wave of lockdowns. Each lid is a little tombstone; each label, smudged by condensation, is a fading epitaph written in Sharpie. I could map my last five years by condiment alone.

Outside, the city is practicing its usual alchemy: turning oxygen into urgency. Somewhere down the block a brake squeals like a hawk, and a dog answers with a howl that contains multitudes—loneliness, territorial pride, the memory of wolves compressed into a 25-pound rescue with cataracts. I love that dog without knowing his name. I love the strangers who walk past my window at 2:17 a.m., arguing in a language I can’t identify, their consonants clacking like pool balls. I love the insomniac upstairs who drops what sounds like a marble every night at 3:05. Maybe it’s an actual marble. Maybe it’s his soul, loosened by gravity.

I once believed the world could be saved by grand gestures—treaties signed on mahogany tables, billion-dollar satellites, manifestos hurled onto stages. Now I suspect salvation hides in smaller currencies: the held elevator, the extra two dollars tipped to the woman whose wrists are rashy from bleach, the text that says simply “I remember how you like your coffee.” We are all amateur locksmiths, fumbling with the tiny mechanisms that keep each other locked or free.

Yesterday I found a Polaroid wedged behind the dresser. My twelve-year-old face squints back at me, freckles like cinnamon on a pale pudding, haircut achieved by placing a bowl over my own head—an act of autodecapitation by boredom. On the back, in my mother’s tidy teacher-hand: “First day of seventh grade, refuses to remove cape.” I’d forgotten the cape: a bath towel safety-pinned at the throat, scarlet flannel faded to the color of dried poppies. I wore it every day for months because I was convinced invisibility was a skill you could learn only if you felt the tug of something heroic at your shoulders. I wanted to be impossible to see except when justice required me. Instead I was impossible to miss, a bony specter clanking through the halls with a towel that smelled of Head & Shoulders and macaroni. No one ever told me capes were ridiculous. They let me audition for vanishment on my own terms. That was the first mercy I can name.

Memory is a thrift store where nothing is priced but everything costs. I roam the aisles and touch the scratched vinyl of my parents’ arguments, smell the cedar of the closet where I hid with the Halloween candy I’d sworn I’d share. I try on a sweater knitted from the sound of my father’s laugh—low, surprised, as if humor were a trespasser he kept deciding not to report. It still fits, itchier than I recall. At the counter the clerk—who looks like me in twenty years if I start drinking seltzer and stop apologizing—bags my selections in translucent plastic that will survive longer than the oceans. I carry them home, these fragments, and fold them into the drawer labeled “evidence.”

There is a crack in the plaster above my desk shaped like the Jordan River if you squint and are generous with metaphor. I watch it the way astronomers watch nebulae, hoping it will teach me how borders widen silently until two banks no longer recognize each other. Some mornings it seems wider; others, it nearly knits shut, a shy zipper. I think of tectonic plates falling in love, the slow collision that builds mountains neither asked for. Geology is just gossip told in millions of years. I wonder what the drywall whispers to the lathe when the lights go out.

I keep a list of obsolete futures: flying cars, silver jumpsuits, meals in pill form, the 4-hour workday, the paperless office, the friendly robot maid. None arrived, yet here we are, carrying supercomputers in pockets while the Arctic dresses itself in flame. Progress is a magician who keeps changing the volunteer in the box; we applaud the trick even as we fail to notice the original assistant has disappeared. I add new lines daily: honesty in politics, a social media that doesn’t monetize shame, the joke my grandfather never finished because he laughed too hard to reach the punch line. Sometimes the list feels like a petition signed by ghosts.

What I’m really doing, I think, is practicing how to say goodbye without using the word. Goodbye is a stone in the mouth; it sinks conversations. Instead I say “take care,” “see you soon,” “send me that recipe,” all of which mean: I hope the world keeps you gentle. I have become a collector of almost-last words. The barista who writes “sunrise” on my cup because I forgot my name tag. The neighbor who apologizes for the smell of curry drifting through the vents, not knowing it reminds me of the one night my mother felt well enough to cook. The voicemail from a friend that ends mid-sentence because service fails under the weight of too much tenderness.

At night I lie very still and pretend I am a library book someone has checked out for the hundredth time. Spine cracked, pages soft as cloth, margins dotted with the fingerprints of strangers who ate lunch while reading me. I imagine them: a teenager on the Q train highlighting the sex scenes, a retired nun underlining passages about despair, a man who smells of sawdust folding an airplane from my 217th page and sailing it into the abyss between stations. Their attention is a form of immortality more durable than ink. I want to be over-due, to incur fines, to be slipped into backpacks and smuggled across borders, to suffer coffee rings like halos. When at last I am too worn to circulate, I want to be sold for a quarter on the library steps, bought by a kid who pays in dimes and sticky hope, who writes inside my cover: This book saved me without knowing my name.

Dawn arrives the way a cat enters a room—tail up, eyes narrowed, pretending it doesn’t need anyone yet making sure you notice. Light pools on the hardwood like milk nobody spilled. I stand in it barefoot, a worshiper at the church of warm floorboards. The refrigerator shifts from hum to hiccup, signaling the compressor’s last breath; someday soon I’ll have to defrost, confront the glacial archives. I open the window. The city exhales steam, coffee, and the faint metal of trolley wheels. Somewhere the dog is finally asleep, the marble-dropper too. I think of the cape I once wore, how it did not make me invisible but did make me feel the exact size of my courage: small, red, ridiculous, and utterly necessary.

A thousand words is not a cage; it is a tide that recedes and leaves behind salt on your lips, a shell the shape of a human ear. You can hold it to the noise and believe the ocean is still talking long after you’ve walked inland. I have used my allotment to inventory the refrigerator of my haunting, to catalog the condiments of postponed joy. When I reach the final period, I will not vanish; I will simply step out of these sentences the way one slips off a coat at a party, leaving it on the bed among others, trusting it will still be there when the music quiets and the lights come up—warm, familiar, smelling faintly of everything I carried through the cold.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
